# Storm Guardian Demo Script (3 minutes)

## Scene 1: The Map (30 sec)

**Screen:** Browser opens to the live demo page — full-screen Leaflet map with weather overlays. Radar tiles are streaming over Oklahoma. A red polygon marks a severe thunderstorm warning.

**Voiceover:**
"This is a live weather map. The radar tiles, the alerts, the model overlays — all generated by a pure-Rust weather server I built from scratch. 50,000 lines of code, 18 crates, zero C dependencies. Standard XYZ tiles that work with Leaflet, Mapbox, Google Maps — anything. But a map doesn't keep you safe at 2 AM. For that, you need an agent."

**Show:** Zoom into Norman, OK. Click a warning polygon. Toggle between radar and HRRR temperature layers to show multiple tile products.

---

## Scene 2: Morning Briefing (40 sec)

**Screen:** Phone showing Telegram. A message arrives from Storm Guardian at 6:00 AM.

**Message content:**
> Good morning! It's 62°F and mostly cloudy in Norman. Today's high will be 78°F with a 40% chance of afternoon thunderstorms. Winds pick up to 20 mph after noon.
>
> Mowing: Get it done this morning — winds are light now but it'll be gusty by lunch.
> Spraying: Not today. Winds above 10 mph by 10 AM and rain likely this afternoon.
>
> Heads up: SPC has a Slight Risk for our area this afternoon. I'll keep watching.

**Voiceover:**
"Every morning at 6 AM, Storm Guardian sends a briefing. It fetched real HRRR model data and NWS forecasts — 50 kilobytes, done in under 2 seconds — and translated everything into advice tailored to what this user actually does. Mowing. Spraying. Not dewpoint or 500mb heights. Answers."

**Show:** Quick flash of terminal — `wx_brief` completing in 1.4 seconds.

---

## Scene 3: Natural Language Question (30 sec)

**Screen:** Phone. User types a message.

**User:** "Can I spray the orchard tomorrow?"

**Screen:** Terminal showing the agent calling `wx_forecast`, checking hourly wind, precip timing.

**Storm Guardian responds:**
> Tomorrow looks good for spraying between 7-11 AM. Winds 5-8 mph from the south, no rain until late evening. Mid-60s — ideal for application.
>
> After noon, winds hit 15-20 mph. Wrap up by lunch. No inversions expected, drift risk is low in the morning window.

**Voiceover:**
"Natural question, specific answer. The agent checked hourly winds, precipitation timing, temperature, inversion risk — and the first time it solved a spray question, it wrote a skill for it. That knowledge persists."

---

## Scene 4: Severe Weather Escalation (50 sec)

**Screen:** Split — phone on left, terminal on right, live map on a second monitor.

**2:00 PM — Cron check fires.**
Terminal shows `wx_alerts` call. SPC upgrades to Moderate Risk.

**Phone notification (YELLOW):**
> A Tornado Watch has been issued for Cleveland County until 10 PM. Conditions support supercells with large hail and a few tornadoes. Keep your phone charged and know where your shelter is.

**4:30 PM — Cron check fires again.**
Terminal shows `wx_severe` call, then `wx_radar`.

**Phone notification (ORANGE):**
> SEVERE THUNDERSTORM WARNING for Cleveland County until 5:15 PM. 70 mph wind gusts and golf-ball hail. Move indoors and stay away from windows.

**Show:** Map updates live — radar tiles refresh, warning polygon appears.

**5:15 PM — Tornado Warning issued.**

**Phone notification (RED):**
> TORNADO WARNING — Take shelter NOW. Radar confirms rotation 12 miles southwest of Norman, moving NE at 35 mph. Interior room, lowest floor. Do not wait.

**5:45 PM — All clear.**

**Phone notification:**
> All clear. The Tornado Warning has expired. Storm has moved east. You're safe.

**Voiceover:**
"Watch the escalation. A watch gets a calm heads-up. A warning gets urgent. A tornado warning tells you exactly what to do. And the entire event — all the radar, all the checks — 15 megabytes. That's what matters when your internet is satellite."

**Show:** Bandwidth counter: 15.2 MB total.

---

## Closing (20 sec)

**Screen:** The architecture diagram — agent on top, platform underneath. Then the Telegram chat showing the day's messages. Then the live map one more time.

**Voiceover:**
"I built a weather platform from scratch — pure Rust, a tile server, SDKs, 20 MCP tools. And I put a Hermes Agent on top of it. Not because I wanted to build a platform. Because my dad lives in rural Oklahoma on satellite internet, and weather apps don't work for him. This does."

**Title card:**
> **Storm Guardian**
> 50K LOC. Pure Rust. Built to keep my family safe.

---

## Technical Notes for Recording

- Use real weather data — pick a recent severe weather day
- The live map at `demo/index.html` is the visual hook; open it first
- Terminal should show actual tool calls and response times
- Phone can be Telegram desktop preview or screen capture
- The map + agent split screen in Scene 4 is the "wow" moment — radar tiles updating live while the agent escalates
- Keep transitions fast — 3 minutes is tight
- The closing should breathe. Don't rush the personal note.
